actions.thought: K2o is a weak hand with no playability or significant equity, especially in a 3-way pot. Folding is the standard professional play from the button to preserve stack depth early in the tournament.

Folded to me in the small blind, I hold T6o, which is a very weak starting hand. While playing from the SB against the BB allows for a wider opening range, T6o remains heavily -EV to play due to its poor equity, lack of suitedness, and significant reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard and mathematically sound decision to preserve stack depth early in the tournament.

I am on the button (first to act 3-handed) with 94s. This is a very weak starting hand, even for a steal attempt. It lacks high card strength, connectivity, and significant flush value. The optimal play is to fold and wait for a better hand.

In a blind-on-blind scenario after the button folds, Ks3s is a strong enough hand to raise for value and fold equity. I will raise to 3x the big blind to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the hand.

I am defending my Big Blind with a suited hand (Qd7d). The Small Blind has raised to 300. Given the pot odds (need 200 to call a 400 pot) and the fact that I am in position for the remainder of the hand, calling is the standard and mathematically sound play to see a flop.

I have the second-best possible flush draw (King-high spade draw with the Ace of spades on board) on an Ace-high board. As the pre-flop aggressor, this board favors my range significantly. I will lead with a continuation bet to capitalize on my range advantage and build the pot in case I improve to a flush, while also giving myself a chance to take down the pot if the opponent missed.

The flop texture (As-Ts-5h) is highly favorable to the Small Blind's pre-flop raising range, which is heavily weighted toward high-card hands and Aces. With Queen-high (Qd7d) and no pair, straight draw, or flush draw, my equity is extremely low against a standard continuation bet. Calling a 300-chip bet into a 900-chip pot with practically no chance of winning at showdown—unless I hit a miracle runner-runner combination—is a negative expectation play. Folding is the most disciplined and effective move to preserve my stack for future hands.

I am on the button with Jd 5d in a three-handed game. While J5s is a relatively weak suited hand, it is strong enough to open for a raise from the button to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot immediately. Standard opening size is around 2.5x the big blind.

Holding Ace-3 offsuit in the small blind against an aggressive button open is not a profitable spot. I am out of position for the remainder of the hand, and my hand is easily dominated by any stronger Ace. While the Ace offers some bluffing potential, the risk of playing a bloated pot out of position against a range that likely includes many pairs and stronger high cards outweighs the benefit of continuing. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Ace-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand to play out of position against a Button raise. While it's slightly ahead of a typical Button stealing range, playing it from the Big Blind is tricky and can lead to being dominated by better Aces or value-owned when we hit a Seven. I prefer to fold here and wait for a stronger holding or better position to get involved.

6-9 offsuit is a marginal hand that lacks high-card strength and connectivity, making it difficult to play profitably even in a 3-handed game on the button. As I am the first to act, folding is the most disciplined and strategic choice to preserve my stack for more favorable opportunities.

Queen-Four suited is a marginal hand, and playing it out of position from the small blind against the big blind is an unnecessary risk this early in the tournament. I'll fold and look for a better spot to invest chips.
Holding 6h2h on the button is a very weak starting hand. Even with the positional advantage, this suited trash is not worth opening. Folding is the optimal play to preserve my stack for better spots.

With the Button folding, I am in the Small Blind with K3o. While not a strong hand, it is sufficient to put pressure on the Big Blind in a blind-versus-blind situation. Raising to 300 allows me to potentially take the blinds immediately or play a pot against a wider range from the Big Blind. I've observed Flashlight fold his Big Blind to a similar raise earlier, so aggression here seems profitable.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Four offsuit, facing a raise from the Small Blind. I am being offered decent pot odds (200 to call into a pot that will be 500) to defend my blind. Since the Small Blind's opening range is typically very wide in this context, King-Four is a reasonable hand to defend with to avoid being exploited by steal attempts. Calling allows me to see a flop with a decent high-card hand.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and have initiative. The flop (Jc-8d-5d) missed my K3o, but it is a board where a continuation bet can often take the pot down, especially since Flashlight has shown a tendency to fold to post-flop aggression in Hand #2. I will bet 300, which is 50% of the pot, to represent a strong hand like a Jack or a high pocket pair and force him to fold his air or weak draws.

Facing a continuation bet on a board (5d 8d Jc) that hits a standard small blind opening range, my King-high is insufficient to continue. I have minimal equity with no pair and only backdoor flush potential. Investing more chips out of position into a range that is likely to bet again on the turn is not a profitable strategy. Folding is the prudent play to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

On the button in a 3-handed game, 98o is a strong enough hand to open-raise for value and pressure. I have been active in previous hands, and continuing this aggression allows me to take down the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate here.

I am in the small blind with 8d, Jh, which is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive button raiser. Flash has been active with raises in multiple previous hands. Calling would put me in a difficult position post-flop without a strong hand range. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision.

Flash has been opening very aggressively on the button. With pocket deuces in the Big Blind and nearly 100 BBs deep, we have excellent implied odds to set-mine. If we hit a set, Flash's high continuation bet frequency will allow us to build a large pot. If we miss, we can easily check-fold on most flops. I am calling the additional 200 to defend and see a flop.

I called pre-flop to set-mine but completely missed this highly coordinated monotone flop (Jc 8c Tc). I have a low pocket pair with no flush draw, so my hand is essentially dead. I will check to the pre-flop raiser and plan to fold to any bet.

I have bottom pair (8s) and an open-ended straight draw on a very dangerous monotone board (Jc-Tc-8c). Since the board is all clubs and I do not have a club in my hand, betting is risky as I could easily be facing a made flush or a high flush draw. By checking back, I control the pot size and get to see a turn card for free, realizing my equity without being forced off the hand by a check-raise.

The turn brings a blank 4h. We have pocket deuces, which is bottom pair on a very coordinated board with three clubs. Since Flash checked behind on the flop, they could be trapping, pot-controlling, or simply giving up with air. Regardless, our hand has almost zero equity against any calling range, and betting as a bluff makes little sense since we hold no blockers to flushes or straights and have no redraw equity. Checking is the only logical play here, and we will fold if Flash makes a bet.

On this monotone board of Jc Tc 8c, I have bottom pair (8s) and an open-ended straight draw (9s 8h needs a Q or 7). However, I do not have a club in my hand, which makes this board extremely dangerous. Pro has checked twice, suggesting weakness or a trap with a made flush. By checking back the turn, I control the pot size and ensure I see the river for free. Betting here is risky because a check-raise would force me to fold my equity, and I have enough showdown value to potentially win if the hand goes check-check to the end or if I improve on a safe river card.

On the river, the board remains very coordinated and our pocket deuces have almost zero showdown value. Since Flash checked the flop and turn, they might be holding a weak pair or missed high cards. However, turning our hand into a bluff makes little sense as we hold no relevant blockers to straights or flushes. Checking to either see a free showdown or fold to any bet is the optimal play.

On the river, the 7s completes a straight for me (7-8-9-10-J). While the board contains three clubs, making a flush possible, the opponent (Pro) has checked three times (flop, turn, and river) after calling pre-flop. This passive line suggests they likely do not have a made flush, as they would typically bet for value or protect their hand on such a coordinated board. I have a very strong hand that beats all one-pair and two-pair combinations. I will place a value bet of roughly half the pot to get called by a Jack, a Ten, or a lower straight, while remaining prepared to fold if faced with a large check-raise, which would heavily signify a flush.

With pocket deuces on a Jc 8c Tc 4h 7s board, our hand has absolutely no showdown value against any realistic value betting or even thin bluffing range. Flash's river bet could easily be a delayed value bet or a stab with a hand that still beats us (like a missed flush draw that paired up, or Ace high). Folding is the only viable option.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in this position. Given the stack depths and the fact that I am in late position, I will open-raise to 300 to attempt to take down the blinds or play a pot with initiative.
Q5 offsuit is a weak hand. Facing a raise from the button and being out of position in the small blind with the big blind still to act, the optimal play is to fold.

Ten-Four offsuit is a very weak hand. Although the pot odds to call from the Big Blind are about 3:1, the hand realizes equity poorly and is difficult to play out of position against a Button open. Given the tournament context and current stack lead, folding is the disciplined choice to avoid unnecessary losses with trash hands.
I am on the Button with 8s 3c, which is an extremely weak, unsuited hand. It has essentially no playability or equity to justify opening the pot. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with QhTd. The Button has folded, making this a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. QTo is a strong starting hand in this configuration. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot, as I have seen the Big Blind fold to several pre-flop raises in earlier hands.

Ten-four offsuit is a bottom-tier hand, even in the big blind. Given the opponent's aggressive raising patterns in previous hands, continuing with such a weak hand out of position against an active opener is not profitable and would lead to difficult post-flop spots. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 7-8 offsuit in a three-handed game. This is a standard hand to open with to put pressure on the blinds, especially given that both Flashlight and Pro have been playing relatively tight against my previous raises. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) is consistent with my previous play and allows me to take control of the pot early or win it pre-flop.

My holding of Ten-Three offsuit is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. Calling against a button open from this position with such a disconnected low-card hand is negative expected value. Given the tournament structure and the need to preserve my stack for better spots, the disciplined decision is to fold and wait for a more playable range.

Flash is raising frequently from the Button, but Q2s is a very weak hand to defend with out of position, even given his wide opening range. Calling will lead to a difficult post-flop situation with a weak kicker, and using this hand as a 3-bet bluff is less optimal than hands with better blockers or connectivity. Folding is the most disciplined and safest choice here.

Ten-eight offsuit is a marginal, uncoordinated hand that does not play well in a three-handed game. While I am on the button, which provides some positional advantage, the strength of the hand is insufficient to justify opening the pot or calling the big blind. Preserving my stack early in this 150-hand tournament is a higher priority than playing mediocre starting hands.

With K8s in the Small Blind and the Button folding, this is a clear opportunity to play aggressively against the Big Blind. K8s is well ahead of a random hand, and opening to 3bb puts pressure on the BB, taking the initiative in a Blind vs Blind scenario.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of threes. Pro (Small Blind) raised to 3 times the big blind. In a blind-versus-blind situation, a small pocket pair is relatively strong and definitely worth seeing a flop with, especially since I will have position throughout the rest of the hand. Calling allows me to set-mine or evaluate the flop texture without inflating the pot too much pre-flop.

The flop comes A-9-2 rainbow, which is an excellent texture for my perceived pre-flop raising range. Although I completely missed the board with K8s, a small continuation bet will apply pressure to the Big Blind and likely fold out a large portion of their defend range, including underpairs, Broadway floats, and better King-highs. A 1/3 pot sizing (200 chips) is highly effective and efficient on this dry, static board texture.

The flop of A-9-2 is very favorable for the Small Blind's pre-flop raising range. My pocket threes are currently a weak pair, and while I have position, the presence of an Ace makes it difficult to continue profitably. Even though the bet size is small (1/3 pot), I have very little equity to improve to the best hand and will likely face further aggression on later streets. Folding here preserves my stack for better opportunities.
Q4s is a weak starting hand, even on the button in a 3-handed game. I prefer to fold and preserve my stack for better stealing opportunities or stronger hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 3h 4c, one of the weakest possible hands. Although the action has folded to me and I only need to put in 50 more to see a flop, 34 offsuit performs very poorly post-flop, especially out of position against the Big Blind. Given my stack size and the early stage of the tournament, there is no need to defend or bluff with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Jack-Nine suited, a strong hand for three-handed play. My strategy so far has been aggressive, and I will continue this by opening with a standard 3x raise to 300. This puts pressure on the blinds while allowing me to utilize my positional advantage post-flop.

59 offsuit is a bottom-tier hand with very little playability. Even though the opponent has been aggressive, calling with such a weak hand out of position (Small Blind) is a negative expected value (-EV) play. It is much better to conserve my stack and wait for a stronger hand or a more favorable position to defend or counter-attack.

We are in the Big Blind with 6c Th, which is a very weak offsuit hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button (Flash), we are significantly behind their opening range and out of position for post-flop play. The best and standard play here is to simply fold and preserve our stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. Given the aggressive nature of the table and my position, opening the pot is the standard and most profitable play to take control of the hand. A raise to 300 chips is consistent with the sizing used by opponents throughout the session.

We are in the Small Blind with 68 suited facing a 3x open from the Button. 68s is too weak to flat from the SB, as it puts us out of position against the preflop raiser and leaves us vulnerable to a squeeze from the Big Blind. The standard play is to fold.

Flashlight raised to 3x from the button. While I have a King, the Deuce kicker is very weak, and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Defending K2o here is marginally unprofitable against a standard button opening range. I'll fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.
52 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Being the first to act on the Button 3-handed, folding is the only profitable play.

I am in the Small Blind with 4h 5c. Pro has folded from the Button. While I have been active in stealing blinds in previous hands, 4-5 offsuit is at the very bottom of my range. Although Flashlight has folded to several of my pre-flop raises recently, raising here with such a weak hand risks being called and playing out of position with minimal equity. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to maintain my chip lead.

I am on the button with 7-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. While I have been playing aggressively and currently hold the chip lead, opening with such a low-equity hand is unnecessary. I will wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

I hold Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium starting hand in a heads-up blind-vs-blind scenario. With the button having folded, I have a significant equity advantage over the Big Blind. A raise to 300 is standard to build the pot and exert pressure, allowing me to potentially take the pot down pre-flop or set up a strong continuation bet post-flop.
95o is an extremely weak hand and faces a 3x raise from the small blind. We should easily fold this garbage hand pre-flop.

I am in the button position with King-Ten offsuit. This is a very strong hand for 3-handed play, well within my opening range for the button. Raising to 300 is a standard open that puts pressure on the blinds and allows me to dictate the betting post-flop, helping me capitalize on my positional advantage.

I hold 6s 3h offsuit in the Small Blind facing a 3bb raise from the Button. This is a very weak hand with practically no playability post-flop or potential to hit a strong hand. Folding is the only viable option.

I have 5s7s in the Big Blind. Flashlight, on the button, has raised to 300. Pro folded the Small Blind. Suited one-gappers like 5s7s are standard defending hands in the Big Blind against a button raise, especially with deep stacks and favorable pot odds (200 to call into a total pot of 650). I will call to see the flop and play from there.

I have a gutshot straight draw with my 7 and 5 on a 9-6-4 board. As the player in the Big Blind who called a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor (Flashlight on the button). This allows me to see how they value their hand and decide whether to continue based on the pot odds and their bet sizing.

The flop of 6h-4h-9d has missed my hand completely, leaving me with only King-high and no draws. Given the board texture, which is favorable for the Big Blind's calling range, checking back is the most disciplined approach. This allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn without unnecessarily bloating the pot with a hand that currently has very little showdown value.

I have an open-ended straight draw (OESD) with 7s5s on a board of 6h4h9d6c. After the flop went check-check, the turn 6c is a card that generally favors my big blind defending range more than the button's opening range. However, checking is the most prudent play to see how Flashlight responds to the board pairing. If he checks back again, I get a free river to realize my equity. If he bets, I have a strong draw to call with.

The board is now 6h-4h-9d-6c, and I hold King-high with no improvement. My opponent checked the turn, following a check on the flop. Given that I have no showdown value and minimal equity, betting is ineffective because it likely won't force a better hand to fold, and I have no value to extract. Checking back is the best strategy to maintain pot control, get to the river for free, and re-evaluate without risking more chips in a hand where I am currently behind.

I have 7-high (5s 7s) on a board of 6h 4h 9d 6c Kh. I missed my straight draw. The action has been check-check on both the flop and the turn, indicating both players have relatively weak ranges. While my 7-high has almost no showdown value, the King of hearts on the river is a card that could have improved Flashlight's button-opening range or completed a flush. I will check and hope for a check-back, as bluffing into a range that could contain many weak pairs or Ace-highs that might call is suboptimal in this small pot.

The board finished 6h-4h-9d-6c-Kh, and I have paired my King. While I now have a pair of Kings, the hand has been checked down by both players on every street. The pot is relatively small, and betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands frequently enough to justify the risk of a check-raise, especially considering that the board has several pairs and potential straight draws that could have connected with my opponent's checking range. Checking back is the most prudent path to showdown, securing the pot without unnecessary risk.
Ten-Eight suited is a solid one-gapper that plays very well post-flop. Being first to act on the Button, this is a standard spot to open-raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position if called.

I have pocket deuces in the small blind. The button (Pro) raised to 300 (3x the big blind). While pocket deuces are the weakest pair and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the stack sizes are deep enough (around 90-100 BBs) to justify calling for set-mining value. The goal is to hit a set on the flop and potentially win a large pot from the button's wide opening range. If I miss the set, I can easily check-fold to aggression on most boards.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Five offsuit. The pot currently contains 700 chips, and it costs 200 for me to call. Given the pot odds of 3.5 to 1 and the fact that I am closing the action pre-flop, calling is the most mathematically sound decision. A5o is a playable hand in this scenario, but raising would likely be overplaying against both an opener and a caller.

On this flop of Qh-5c-3s, I have missed my set with pocket deuces. In a three-way pot and being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) and the big blind (Flashlight), the best course of action is to check. Pocket deuces have very little showdown value here and almost no potential to improve unless I hit one of the two remaining deuces. I will look to fold to most bets as this hand is not strong enough to continue in a multi-way pot.

I have flopped bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a relatively dry board in a three-way pot. Given that the board texture is not very dynamic, there are limited draws for my opponents to have, but my hand is also not strong enough to value bet against two opponents. Checking is the standard play to preserve the pot size and realize equity, keeping the option to fold if the original pre-flop raiser (Pro) makes a significant bet, or check along to see a turn card for free.

The flop of Qh-5c-3s is very dry, and we completely missed it, holding just Ten-high with weak backdoor draws. Although our perceived pre-flop range connects well with the Queen, betting into two opponents with zero equity is risky, as one of them could easily hold a Queen, a pocket pair, or decide to float. Checking behind controls the pot size and allows us to see a free turn card, where we can evaluate if we pick up any equity like a spade draw or a gutshot before committing more chips.

The turn 4c has significantly improved my hand by giving me an open-ended straight draw (A-2-3-4-5 or 2-3-4-5-6). Since the flop was checked around by all players, including the pre-flop aggressor, it suggests that no one has a particularly strong hand. In a multi-way pot and being out of position, I will check again to realize my equity and potentially see a free river card. If an opponent bets, I will evaluate whether to call based on the pot odds and the potential to win a larger pot if I hit my straight on the river.

With bottom pair (5s) on a board that has now connected with straight possibilities (2 and 6) after the 4c turn, my hand remains a marginal bluff-catcher. Since the flop checked around, it is highly likely that no one has a strong Queen or better. Checking allows me to control the pot size and hopefully realize my equity to showdown without inflating the pot unnecessarily with a hand that is vulnerable to many turn and river cards.

With both opponents checking the flop and now the turn, their ranges are looking very weak, likely capped at Ace-high, weak underpairs, or completely missed overcards. Although my Ten-high has almost zero equity, this is a prime spot for a delayed continuation bet as a bluff. Betting around half the pot applies enough pressure to fold out their weak holdings, allowing me to win an orphaned pot.

On the turn, the 4c has given me an open-ended straight draw (A-2-3-4-5 or 2-3-4-5-6), totaling 8 outs. Pro has bet 450 into a pot of 900 after the flop was checked around. The pot odds are 3:1 (450 to win a current pot of 1350), requiring about 25% equity to call profitably. While my direct equity from 8 outs is approximately 17.4%, the implied odds are significant; if I hit my straight on the river, I am likely to extract further value from Pro's range, which likely includes top-pair Queens or middle-pair hands that checked the flop. Additionally, my straight would be the nuts if a 6 falls. Therefore, calling to realize my equity is the most sound play.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a board that has become connected. The 4c on the turn completed a potential straight draw for anyone holding a 2 or a 6. However, getting 5-to-1 pot odds (450 to win 2250) makes calling mathematically justifiable. I also possess a gutshot straight draw (needing a 2 for a wheel). Calling allows me to reach the river to see if the board improves or if the betting action clarifies the relative strength of my opponents' hands.

On the river 6d, the board now reads Qh-5c-3s-4c-6d. My pocket deuces have improved to a 6-high straight (2-3-4-5-6). However, the board shows a four-card straight (3-4-5-6), meaning any opponent holding a 7 has a higher 7-high straight (3-4-5-6-7). Given that there are two opponents still in the hand and the pot was multi-way on the turn, there is a distinct possibility that someone is holding a 7 (e.g., 77, 87, 76s, or even A7). By checking, I can control the size of the pot and see how the other two players react. If a bet occurs, I will evaluate the size and the player's tendencies before deciding whether to call for showdown value, but leading out into two opponents on such a coordinated board is risky.

The board texture is [Qh, 5c, 3s, 4c, 6d], which is highly connected and enables straights with any 2 or 7. My pair of fives is vulnerable to a wide range of hands, including any Queen, any straight, or better kicker combinations. Since Pro showed strength on the turn by raising and there are still two opponents to contend with, checking is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and reach showdown without risking more chips on a weak hand.

Both opponents calling the turn indicates they have some piece of the board or a strong draw. The river puts a 4-card straight on the board (3-4-5-6), meaning any 7 or 2 makes a straight. Bluffing into two players on this highly coordinated board is extremely risky, as it's very likely at least one of them holds a 7, a 2, or a stubborn Queen that they won't fold. With just Ten-high and zero showdown value, checking and surrendering the pot is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with T6o. While 3-handed play warrants a wider opening range, T6o is still near the bottom of the range and doesn't play well post-flop if called. Given my current chip lead and the fact that both opponents (Flashlight and Pro) have shown willingness to defend or contest pots recently, I will opt for a disciplined fold to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable hand or situation.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (4-2 offsuit). Even though the button has folded, meaning I am heads-up against the big blind, playing such a low-equity hand out of position is negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my stack.

I am dealt pocket aces, the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Being on the button, I should take control of the hand immediately by raising. A standard raise to 300 chips is consistent with previous play at this table and helps me build the pot while potentially narrowing the field.
I am holding T6o in the Small Blind. This is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a pre-flop raise from the Button, the correct and standard play is to fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs 4s. The Button (Flashlight) raised to 300, and the Small Blind folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650. This gives me pot odds of roughly 3.25:1 (requiring about 31% equity). Suited Queens generally have enough equity against a standard button opening range to justify a call, especially with the potential for flushes or straight draws post-flop. I'll call to see the flop.

I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor on an Ace-high board. My hand (Qs 4s) has completely missed the flop, and I have no real draws or backdoor equity to speak of. The standard play here is to check to the raiser and evaluate their action, likely folding to any significant bet as I have no showdown value or potential to improve significantly.

I have flopped top set with pocket aces on a board with two diamonds. I need to value bet to build the pot and protect my hand against potential flush draws. A bet of 350 represents just over half the pot and is appropriately sized to extract value from any weaker Ace, a pair of eights, or straight/flush draws.

I have missed the flop entirely with my Qs 4s on a board of Ad 8d 5h. The Ace-high board favors the pre-flop aggressor's range significantly. Since I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw, I have very little equity to continue in this hand. Calling a continuation bet out of position with just Queen-high is a losing play, and a bluff-raise is too risky given the board texture. I will fold.
T3s is a weak starting hand, especially from the Button when facing two players left to act in the blinds. The best play is to fold and save chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Ten of diamonds, a very strong hand for three-handed play. Since the Button has folded, I am head-up against the Big Blind. I will raise to my standard sizing of 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot with a high-equity hand.

I hold 6c 2h, which is an extremely weak hand with very little playability. Even though I have to call 200 chips into a 400-chip pot to see the flop, the long-term expectation of defending the big blind with such a dominated hand is poor. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a stronger hand to engage with, especially against an aggressive opponent.

I am on the button with Jack-Five offsuit. While I have been playing aggressively and opening many hands to 2.5x or 3x the big blind, J5o is at the very bottom of my range and doesn't play well post-flop. Given my active image, I expect my opponents might start defending their blinds more light, so I will fold this junk hand and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

With Ace-high suited in the small blind and the button having folded, I have a favorable position against the big blind. Raising allows me to capitalize on the opportunity to steal the blinds or take the initiative in the hand, leveraging my Ace to potentially win the pot pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with A7o facing a raise from the Small Blind after the Button folded. A7o is well ahead of a typical Small Blind stealing range, but isn't quite strong enough to comfortably 3-bet for value or want to play a massive inflated pot pre-flop. Calling to play in position is a solid, profitable decision that keeps my opponent's wider range in the hand.

I raised pre-flop and am now the aggressor in this hand. The flop comes Queen-high, which is a board that hits my range well as a pre-flop raiser. Although I only have Ace-high, a continuation bet is standard here to represent strength and potentially win the pot immediately if my opponent missed. A bet of 300 (half pot) puts pressure on the Big Blind and maintains the initiative.

I hold top two pair, Aces and Kings with a Queen kicker, which is a very strong hand on this board. I hold Ace-King offsuit, a premium hand. Pro opened from the button, which is likely a wide opening range. I should 3-bet to take the initiative, maximize value, and discourage further aggression. Raising to 900 puts pressure on the button and allows me to define their range effectively.

I hold the nut flush draw on a paired 5-5-9 board in a three-way pot. The pot odds of 4:1 (calling 300 into a 1200 pot) make this an easy call. With 8c 6c on a 5d 5h 9h board, I only have a weak gutshot straight draw. The pre-flop raiser has continued with a bet and the small blind has already called. Drawing to a non-nut straight in a multi-way pot here is unprofitable, so folding is the best play.

I am out of position in a three-way pot that has now narrowed to heads-up on the turn. I have flopped the nut flush draw and picked up top pair (Aces) on the turn. I am out of position in a three-way pot that has now gone to the river. On the river, the board is 5d-5h-9h-Ad-Jd. I have Ten-high and zero showdown value. Flashlight called my flop continuation bet and has since checked the turn and river. I am in the Big Blind with 9d 7d, which is a solid suited connector. The Button has folded, and the Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. I have the nut flush draw along with two overcards to the board. This gives me massive equity. A continuation bet of half the pot (300) allows me to build the pot for when I hit, fold out some of the opponent's weaker holdings or floats, and maintain the betting lead.

On a board of Jd 8c 7c, I have bottom pair (7d) and an inside straight draw (a nine and a ten would make a straight, but specifically I need a ten for the open-ended or just a ten and a six for various combinations; actually, with 9-7 on J-8-7, any 10 gives me an open-ended straight draw, and a 6 gives me an open-ended straight draw). The 4 of spades on the turn is a complete blank. I still have the nut flush draw and two overcards, giving me massive equity against almost any holding. This semi-bluff builds the pot for when I hit my flush or overcards, while giving me a strong chance to win the pot uncontested right now.

On a board of Jd 8c 7c 4s, I have bottom pair (sevens) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 10). Pro is leading for half-pot (600 into 1200). To call, I need 25% equity. The river is a blank for my hand, leaving me with just Ace-King high as my flush draw and overcards missed. Checking allows me to either win at showdown against a worse missed draw or safely fold to a bet without risking more chips.

On the river board of Jd-8c-7c-4s-6d, I have a pair of sevens (9-7 on board J-8-7-4-6). The board has become very coordinated, with any 5 or 10 completing a straight. My pair of sevens has some showdown value against missed draws, but it is unlikely to be the best hand if Pro has any piece of the board. However, betting as a bluff to try and push him off a Jack or an 8 is risky, as my hand does have marginal showdown value and Pro might not fold a Jack given the pot size. The most prudent professional play is to check behind, take the showdown, and minimize losses or win against a pure bluff.

I have a massive chip lead over both opponents (17,925 vs ~6,000 stacks). While I could raise to put pressure on Flashlight, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see a flop with an Ace-high hand that has decent showdown value and some nut potential if an Ace or straight cards fall. Folding is too tight, and raising might force out hands I dominate while inviting a shove from better hands. I will call the 200.

On this flop of 3d-9d-9s, I have Ace-high. This board is quite dry but has a flush draw. As the Big Blind who called a button raise, I will check to the initial aggressor, Flashlight. My Ace-high has good showdown value and I might be ahead if Flashlight is continuation-betting with air. Checking allows me to see how they react and decide whether to call a bet or take the pot down later if they check back.

I have flopped trips with a 9, which is a very strong hand on this board. With trips, I want to build a pot and extract value from hands like a lower pair, a 3, or potentially a flush draw since there are two diamonds on the board. Checking would give a free card, which is not ideal when I have such a strong holding. Therefore, betting is the best strategic play to continue maintaining the initiative and extracting value.

On a board of 3d-9d-9s, I have Ace-high with a backdoor straight draw. Flashlight's bet of 350 into 650 is a standard continuation bet on a paired board. In a three-handed game, my Ace-high is currently ahead of all their bluffs and has significant showdown value. Folding would be too weak. While I could raise, calling allows Flashlight to continue bluffing with weaker hands on future streets and keeps the pot controlled. I will call to see a turn.

The Queen of Diamonds is a very coordinated card. It completes a possible diamond flush and provides an overcard to the board that hits many button-opening ranges (like KQ, QJ, QT). Having only Ace-high with no diamonds, my hand strength has significantly diminished. I will check to the aggressor; if Flashlight bets again on this texture, it is highly likely they have improved or have a made hand, and I will likely look to fold.

I have trips (9s), which is a very strong hand. The turn card, Queen of Diamonds, completes a possible flush draw for the opponent if they hold two diamonds. However, I must bet for value against weaker holdings (such as a 9, a 3, or a Queen) and to deny equity to any potential draws that might be fishing for the river. Checking would be too passive given the strength of my hand. A bet of around half the pot (700 chips) should be sufficient to extract value while maintaining control of the hand.

On the turn, the Queen of Diamonds is an extremely unfavorable card for my hand. It completes the diamond flush and provides an overcard to the board that likely hits the button's opening range. With only Ace-high and no diamonds in my hand, I have no draw and very little showdown value against a player betting into me for the second time. Flashlight's sizing (700 into a pot of 1350) indicates strength or at least a semi-bluff with a high diamond, both of which I am losing to. I will fold to preserve my stack.

K8s is a perfectly fine hand to open from the Button. It has suited and high card potential, and raising puts pressure on the blinds to fold, potentially winning the pot right now. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd 2d. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. While my hand is suited, it is quite weak (Queen-deuce). Pro's stack is relatively short (50.5 big blinds), and calling from the small blind with a weak hand often puts me in a difficult position post-flop, especially against a player who has shown aggression. Given the stack sizes and the hand strength, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

Holding Ace-King offsuit in the Big Blind against a button raise, I have a strong premium hand. 3-betting is the optimal play to take control of the hand, build a pot with my superior equity, and potentially force out weaker hands. A raise to 1000 is an appropriate 3-bet sizing given the current stack sizes and pot.

While K8s is a fine hand to open from the Button as a steal, it does not play well against a 3-bet from the Big Blind. Our opponent's 3-betting range likely consists of stronger Kings (AK, KQ, KJ), strong Aces, and pocket pairs that dominate our hand. Calling would commit a significant portion of our remaining stack (700 out of 4750) with a marginal holding out of position relative to the aggressor's perceived strength. The most prudent move is to fold and wait for a better spot to invest our chips.

I am on the button with A3o. This is a standard opening hand in three-handed play to put pressure on the blinds, especially given my large chip lead (over 17k). A raise to 300 has been my standard opening size throughout the session, which allows me to take control of the pot early or win the blinds outright.

I am in the small blind with suited connectors (7c6c). Suited connectors have good implied odds, especially in a raised pot where I can potentially hit a straight or flush. Calling the 250 chip raise is mathematically sound given the pot odds and my stack size, allowing me to see a flop cheaply.
I hold 32 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, folding is the only strategically sound option.

The flop of 5s-7s-6h gives me two pair (7s and 6s), which is a very strong hand. However, the board is coordinated with flush and straight draw possibilities. I should bet to protect my equity and extract value from any overpairs or draws that my opponent might hold. A bet of 350, roughly half the pot, is appropriate to maintain control.

On a flop of 5s-7s-6h, the board is highly coordinated and significantly favors the range of the Small Blind caller. My Ace-high (Ac3d) has very little equity here and no significant draws. The opponent's lead (donk bet) indicates they likely connected with this low, connected texture. Given my substantial chip lead and the poor board fit, there is no reason to continue or attempt a bluff. Folding is the most prudent play.

With Queen-Jack offsuit, I have a strong enough hand to open from the button. Raising to 300 is a standard move to take the initiative, put pressure on the blinds, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or play in position.

AJs is a strong hand against a Button open. 3-betting from the Small Blind to 1000 puts pressure on Flashlight, discourages the Big Blind from entering the pot, and gives us the initiative out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 2c, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight opened to 300 from the button and Pro (the Small Blind) has 3-bet to 1000. Pro is also sitting on a relatively short stack of 3950. Calling a 3-bet with 82o is out of the question as it is one of the worst hands in poker and lacks any post-flop playability or equity. Folding is the only viable strategic choice to preserve my stack.

I opened with Queen-Jack suited on the button and faced a 3-bet from the Small Blind. With 7725 in my stack and 700 to call, the pot odds are decent to continue. QJs plays very well in position post-flop, allowing me to navigate the hand against the Small Blind's 3-bet range. Folding is too tight given the position, and a 4-bet would commit too many chips with a marginal hand against a likely strong range. I will call to see the flop.

On a K-K-Q board, my perceived 3-betting range has a massive advantage. Although my AJs didn't connect strongly, I have a gutshot to the Broadway straight and an overcard. A small continuation bet of around 25% pot will fold out many of the opponent's underpairs (JJ-88) and missed floats while keeping the pot manageable. If called, I still have equity with my straight draw.

I have second pair with Queen-Jack on a board of King-King-Queen. The opponent's bet is small (500 into 2600), suggesting it could be a probe bet with an Ace-high hand or a weak pocket pair, though it could also be a King. With such favorable pot odds and my position, calling is the most rational decision to keep the pot controlled and see how the opponent proceeds on the turn. Raising would likely only be called by better hands, and folding is too weak given the pot size and the small bet sizing.

The turn brings an Ace, giving me two pair (Aces and Kings with a Queen kicker). While this improves my hand, the board texture is extremely dangerous. Any Ace (AQ, AJ, AT) simply chops with my hand since the board's Queen plays as the kicker (A-A-K-K-Q). Hands that beat me include JT (which just made a Broadway straight), AK, KQ, and QQ (which all have full houses). Betting here accomplishes little, as worse hands like JJ or TT will likely fold, and I will only be called by hands that chop or beat me. Checking for pot control is the safest and most logical play, allowing me to evaluate his action and potentially see a cheap showdown.

I have two pair (Kings and Queens) on a board of K-K-Q-A. The Ace on the turn is a very threatening card that hits the pre-flop 3-better's range significantly (AK, AQ, AA). Since Pro checked, betting doesn't offer much value as I'm likely only called by better hands, and I don't want to inflate the pot against a potentially stronger range. Checking behind is the best way to control the pot size and reach a showdown to see what the river brings.

On the river, my hand is two pair (Aces and Kings with a Queen kicker playing the board). The board texture is extremely heavy, and any King, Queen, or JT beats me. Betting here has zero expected value since worse hands like pocket Jacks or missed diamond draws will simply fold, while I will only be called or raised by hands that either chop (like another Ace) or beat me (like a straight or full house). Checking is the safest and most optimal play, allowing me to reach a cheap showdown and potentially induce a bluff from a busted draw.

I have two pair, Kings and Queens with an Ace kicker. The board is quite connected, and the Ace on the turn strengthened any range containing an Ace (like A-x). 'Pro' checked on the river, which suggests they either have a weak hand, missed a draw, or are potentially trapping with a boat. Betting here likely only gets called by hands that beat me (like A-K, K-x, or a Full House) or folds out everything I am currently beating. Checking behind allows me to go to showdown safely and collect the pot if my two pair is good, without risking more chips against a stronger range or a trap.
Jack-Eight offsuit is a weak hand to open from the button. There's no reason to invest chips with this holding, so folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s 7c. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. While I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, 75 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given my significant chip lead, there is no reason to risk chips or enter a potentially tricky post-flop situation with such a low-equity hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with a pocket pair (5s, 5h). In a three-handed game, any pocket pair is a strong opening hand. I have a significant chip lead over both opponents. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and pressure the blinds.

Flash is on the button and has been aggressive with opening raises. Ace-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to contest this raise, but playing out of position in the Small Blind suggests that flat-calling is superior to 3-betting. This allows me to see a flop while keeping the pot size manageable and potentially outplaying Flash post-flop if I connect with the board.

I am in the Big Blind with 9To. There is a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind. 9To is too weak to play out of position against two players, even with the decent pot odds. I will fold to preserve my stack for better spots.

The flop of King-Jack-Six with two hearts missed my Ace-Nine completely. While I hold the Ace of hearts as a potential back-door flush draw, my hand is currently only Ace-high and I am out of position. Against an aggressive opponent like Flash, there is no value in leading out. Checking is the standard play to evaluate Flash's action; if they bet, I will likely fold to avoid further investment in a weak hand.

I opened pre-flop with 5s5h and was called by Flashlight in the small blind. The flop comes Kc Jh 6h. This is a very coordinated board that hits a lot of a defending range (any K, J, or heart draws). I have a weak pair that is unlikely to be the best hand if called or raised. Flashlight checked to me. While a continuation bet is standard, checking back here allows me to control the pot size with a marginal made hand and see a turn for free. It also avoids building a pot where I am likely behind or facing a semi-bluff. I will check.

The board has three hearts, and I hold the Nine of Hearts, giving me a flush draw. Flash checked back the flop, which indicates a range that is likely capped or weak (weak King, middle pair, or a draw that didn't want to bet). Betting now applies pressure and allows me to potentially take down the pot immediately, or build value/fold equity. My Ace of Clubs also provides some protection if a club comes, but the primary goal is to capitalize on Flash's passivity and the scary board texture to induce a fold.

Flashlight led into me after the third heart hit the turn. My pocket fives are now significantly behind most of Flashlight's defending range. While I hold the five of hearts, it is the lowest possible heart, meaning any flush I make is vulnerable to being dominated by higher flushes already in the opponent's range (like Ah, Qh, or Th). Since I checked back the flop and showed weakness, Flashlight's bet represents either a made hand (a King, a Jack, or a completed flush) or a semi-bluff with a higher heart. Given my massive stack lead and the poor equity of my hand, I will make the disciplined fold and look for a better spot.

I am on the button with a suited broadway hand (QhTh), which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. Raising to 300 is the standard opening size observed in this tournament and allows me to leverage my position to potentially win the pot pre-flop or take the initiative into the flop.

I am in the Small Blind with K5o facing a 3x raise from the Button. K5o is a very weak hand to defend with out of position, often dominated and rarely flopping well. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision here.

I am in the big blind holding 4h 2d, which is an extremely weak hand. The button, Flashlight, has raised to 300. Given that I have a very large stack and this hand has zero post-flop playability against a standard opening range, there is no reason to defend the blind or attempt a bluff out of position here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Holding Nine-Deuce offsuit (9c 2d), which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Being the first to act on the Button, this is an easy fold. I need to preserve my chips for better starting hands.

I have been dealt pocket Kings, which is the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Since the button (Pro) has folded, I am in the small blind facing the big blind (Flashlight). It is standard to raise here to build the pot and protect my hand against random cards the big blind might be holding. A raise to 300 is consistent with my previous betting patterns and should either take the pot down now or set up a larger pot for post-flop play where I have a significant equity advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 9s, which is a very weak, uncoordinated hand. Given the poor quality of these cards and the fact that I would be playing out of position against Flash, who has shown significant aggression throughout the tournament, folding is the most disciplined and profitable play.

I am on the button with QJ offsuit, which is a strong opening hand in a three-handed game. Raising here allows me to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the shorter-stacked blinds. A standard raise to 300 chips is consistent with my previous betting patterns.

7-10 offsuit is a weak hand to defend with out of position, especially facing an aggressive button open from Flash. Folding is the most disciplined and prudent action to maintain my stack for more favorable situations.
Holding 95o in the Big Blind facing a standard button raise is an easy fold. The hand is disconnected, offsuit, and highly unprofitable to play out of position against an aggressive opponent.

Holding Ace-Six offsuit on the button, this is a favorable position to open the betting and attempt to steal the blinds. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing to take the initiative pre-flop and put pressure on the blinds.

A7s is a very reasonable hand to 3-bet from the Small Blind against a Button open. It blocks strong Aces, has good playability post-flop if called, and can often pick up the pot right here. I'll 3-bet to 1000 to apply pressure to Flashlight's likely wide BTN opening range.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qs 6c. Flashlight opened from the button for 3x, and Pro 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1000. Q6o is a very weak hand to defend in a 3-bet pot, especially when out of position against one player and likely facing a strong range from the 3-better. Given my large chip lead, there is no reason to commit 900 more chips with a hand that is likely dominated or far behind. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I opened the button with A6o, and Pro 3-bet from the small blind. While I have position, A6o is dominated by most of Pro's 3-betting range (typically strong broadway cards or pairs). Calling 700 into a 1400 pot with a weak Ace is unlikely to be profitable long-term, especially out of position relative to the 3-better's range. It is best to fold, save the chips, and wait for a stronger holding.

87o is a weak offsuit connector. While we are on the button, there is little incentive to open this hand given our stack size and the risk of being called or 3-bet by the blinds. Folding is the most straightforward and solid play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Queen suited (Ad Qd), a premium hand. The Button (Pro) has folded. The pot currently consists of the Small Blind and Big Blind. With such a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind situation, I want to build a pot and exert pressure on Flashlight. Standard procedure here is a raise. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a consistent sizing I have used in previous hands (e.g., Hand #2, #5, #8) and is effective for isolating or getting value.

I am in the big blind with 5c 7c, a suited connector hand that plays reasonably well post-flop. Facing a raise to 300 from the small blind, the pot offers me 2:1 pot odds to call. Given that I have already invested 100 chips, calling 200 to see a flop is a standard defensive play in this position, especially against an aggressive opponent like Flash. I will call to see the flop.

I have Ad Qd on a 7s 2h 7h flop. This is a very favorable board for the pre-flop aggressor because it is dry and paired, making it difficult for the Big Blind to have connected solidly. I have two overcards and the Ace of hearts, which provides a backdoor flush draw potential along with the nut flush draw if it turns a heart. Flashlight called my pre-flop raise, which often indicates a range of medium cards, small pairs, or suited connectors. Since I have range advantage here, a continuation bet of 50% pot is standard to take down the pot immediately or get value from weaker hands that might peel, like high-card hands or smaller pairs that are unsure where they stand.

I have flopped trips with 7s 7h and my 7c. This is a very strong hand on this board. Flash is an aggressive player and likely continuation betting. Calling is better than raising here because I want to keep Flash's bluffing range in the pot and let him continue betting on the turn, extracting more value as the hand progresses.

The turn card (3h) completes a potential flush and I do not hold a heart in my hand (Ad Qd). While I was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, this board has become significantly more dangerous for my Ace-high. Flashlight calling the flop suggests they have a piece of the board, a pocket pair, or a draw. By checking, I am looking to control the size of the pot and see how the opponent reacts to the flush-completing card. If they check back, I may still have the best hand or can reconsider a value/bluff bet on the river depending on the card.

I have flopped and now have turned trips with my 7c matching the 7s and 7h on the board. Flash checked on the turn after having been the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop. This board texture (7s 2h 7h 3h) has potential flush draws. Betting is the optimal play here to get value from any smaller pairs, draws, or weaker holdings Flash might be holding. I will bet 600, half of the 1200 pot, to extract value and protect against potential draws.

On a board of 7s-2h-7h-3h, the turn has completed a potential heart flush and I do not hold a heart in my hand. After I checked, Flashlight bet 600 into a 1200 pot. Given that Flashlight called both my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, their range is likely to include pocket pairs, 7x hands, or heart draws that have now arrived. Ace-Queen high has very little showdown value here and almost no equity against Flashlight's likely calling range which has now improved. Calling here would likely result in having to fold to a river bet or losing to any pair or better. As the chip leader, I will opt for the disciplined fold and look for a more favorable spot.

I am on the button with Td 6d. This is a speculative suited hand that can play well in a multi-way pot or as a steal attempt. Given my current stack lead (16k vs 7k and 6k), I want to maintain pressure. Opening for 3x (300) is consistent with my previous play and allows me to take down the blinds or see a flop with position if called.

Flash (Button) has opened with a raise. Holding 6h 5h in the Small Blind, I have a decent suited connector that plays well in multi-way pots and has good implied odds. Calling the 250 chip deficit is a standard defensive play, although I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Given the stack depths, this is a reasonable speculative call to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with K4o, a weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, calling would put me out of position with a weak, easily dominated hand. Folding is the most profitable decision here.

I am out of position against the button raiser on a dry board (Qd 9s 2h). My hand (6h 5h) has minimal equity here, only a very weak backdoor flush draw. Betting out of position into a range that likely connects well with this board (containing many broadway combinations) would be a mistake. Checking is the standard play, and I am prepared to fold if my opponent bets.

On a board of Qd-9s-2h, I have a back-door diamond flush draw and ten-high. This board hits an opening range better than a defending range from the blinds. Flashlight has checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. A continuation bet (C-bet) of roughly half the pot (350) is effective here to fold out hands like middle pairs, small pairs, or weak draws from Flashlight, while capitalizing on my positional advantage and range lead.

The board (Qd 9s 2h) is very dry and highly favorable to the button's opening range, which likely contains many Queen, King, and Ace high combinations. My hand (6h 5h) has no pair, no straight draw, and only a weak, non-nut backdoor flush draw, providing virtually zero equity to continue in this pot. Calling 350 chips into a 1050 chip pot with such a weak holding would be a mathematical mistake, so I must fold.

I am holding 7-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Regardless of position, there is no strategic value in playing this hand against two opponents. Folding is the only disciplined play to preserve my stack.
I am in the Small Blind with 92o, an extremely weak hand. Although it is folded to me and I only have the Big Blind to beat, 92o has almost no playability or equity. Folding is the optimal decision.

I am on the Button with 7c 5h, which is a very weak offsuit one-gapper. This hand has very low equity and poor playability post-flop. In a 3-handed game, the Button should open a wide range, but 75o falls well outside of any standard opening range. The optimal and standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am the chip leader in the Small Blind. With 87o, I have a semi-connected hand that can play reasonably well against a single opponent. Since the Button (Pro) folded, I will put pressure on the Big Blind (Flashlight) by raising to 300. This has been my standard opening size throughout the tournament and allows me to take down the blinds pre-flop or set up a continuation bet on the flop if called, utilizing my stack advantage.

I hold a very weak hand (4-3 offsuit) in the big blind against a raise from the small blind. While the pot odds are somewhat tempting, 4-3 offsuit has very poor playability and is dominated by almost any range Flash would be raising with. Calling here would likely result in playing a bloated pot out of position with a marginal hand, which is a losing strategy long-term. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with KJs, a very strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. My stack is significant, and both opponents have around 65-68 big blinds. I want to build the pot and exert positional pressure. A standard open to 3x (300 total) is appropriate here to take control of the hand.

My hand (5-7 offsuit) is very weak and playing from the small blind puts me at a significant positional disadvantage throughout the hand against an aggressive button raiser. Calling 250 to potentially play a mediocre hand out of position is not mathematically sound or strategically advantageous. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a stronger starting hand.

A9 offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend from the Big Blind against a Button open, especially given Flash's high opening frequency. Calling is standard here to see a flop and evaluate, as 3-betting might force us into a difficult spot if we get 4-bet, while folding is too tight against a likely wide range.

I missed this highly coordinated low board (6h 5d 4h) entirely with my Ace-Nine offsuit. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard and safest play is to check. If Flash bets, I will have an easy fold, as I have no draws and only Ace-high.

I have two overcards (King and Jack) and a backdoor flush draw on a very coordinated board (6h-5d-4h). The board texture heavily favors the Big Blind's range, which includes many sets, two pairs, and straight draws. However, I am in position and 'Pro' (the Big Blind) has checked to me. A continuation bet here serves to fold out some of his better high-card hands or weak draws, though I must be wary of a check-raise. Betting 300 (roughly half-pot) is a standard sizing to maintain pressure while leaving room to navigate turns.

I missed this highly coordinated flop completely, having only Ace-high with no straight or flush draws. My opponent has continuation bet, and given the board texture (which strongly favors straight and flush draws or made hands), defending with just Ace-Nine offsuit out of position is a losing play. The optimal decision is to fold.

King-Five offsuit is a weak hand that does not warrant an investment from the button. Given the strength of the cards and the position, folding is the most disciplined and prudent action to preserve my stack.

AQo is a premium starting hand. Since it's folded to me in the Small Blind, I have a very strong holding against the Big Blind's random hand. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard open to take the initiative, build the pot for value, or take down the blinds right away.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Nine suited, a strong hand for defending against a Small Blind open. The pot is offering me 2:1 odds to call (200 more into a pot of 400). Pro is the shortest stack at the table, and while I have a significant chip lead, Q9s plays well post-flop and I will have positional advantage for the rest of the hand. I will call to see a flop and realize my hand's equity.

The flop of 7c Td Jc is extremely wet and coordinates heavily with the Big Blind's pre-flop calling range, which includes many broadway hands, suited connectors, and club draws. While I have two overcards and a gutshot to the nut straight (any King), betting into this texture out of position is risky, as I'm likely to get called or raised by better hands and strong draws. Checking allows me to exercise pot control, evaluate my opponent's action, and hopefully see a free turn card to realize my equity.

On this flop of 7c-Td-Jc, I have an open-ended straight draw with my Queen-Nine of diamonds. Pro, the pre-flop raiser from the Small Blind, has checked to me. This board texture is quite connected and likely hits a lot of a defending range, which might be why Pro is being cautious. I have significant equity with the straight draw and one overcard. By checking back, I can realize my equity for free and potentially see a turn card that improves my hand or gives me more information about Pro's holding. Given I have the chip lead and positional advantage, taking a free card here is a safe and effective play.

The turn brings an Ace, giving me top pair with a premium kicker, to go along with my gutshot straight draw. Since the flop checked through, it's less likely my opponent has a monster. Betting here gets value from weaker pairs, broadway draws, and club draws that might call. A half-pot bet of 300 sizes well to charge draws while keeping worse hands in.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd9d, holding an open-ended straight draw (K or 8 makes a straight) on a board of 7c-Td-Jc-Ah. Pre-flop, Pro (Small Blind) raised and I called. The flop went check-check. On the turn, the Ace of hearts appeared, and Pro has led out for 300 into a 600 pot. This Ace is a good card for the pre-flop aggressor's range, but it doesn't change my open-ended draw. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call (300 to win a total pot of 1200). With 8 outs to a straight, my equity is roughly 17% to hit on the river, which is slightly below the 25% required by direct odds. However, I have significant implied odds if I hit, especially against an Ace, and Pro's turn lead could be a delayed continuation bet or a semi-bluff. Calling allows me to realize my equity in position.

The river 6d is a blank that misses the club flush draws and most straight draws (like KQ, Q9, 89). I hold top pair with a strong Queen kicker. While I have a strong hand, a value bet might only get called by better hands like two pair, while folding out all of my opponent's missed draws and weaker pairs (Jx, Tx). By checking, I exercise pot control, ensure I get to showdown cheaply, and give my opponent the opportunity to bluff with their busted draws.

On the river, the board is 7c-Td-Jc-Ah-6d. I have Qd-9d, which means I missed my open-ended straight draw (K or 8). Pro, the small blind, checked to me after leading on the turn when the Ace appeared. While his check might indicate weakness, my Q-high has almost no showdown value. However, Pro's turn bet suggests he likely has at least a pair or was semi-bluffing. Since I have a significant chip lead and the pot is relatively small (1200), I will check this back rather than attempt a high-variance bluff against a professional player who might be looking to induce a bet or simply take a marginal hand to showdown.

I am on the button with 2c 7c, which is an incredibly weak and disconnected hand. Despite being suited, it has essentially no playability or high card value. Folding is the only reasonable decision here.

I am in the Small Blind with K7o. The Button (Pro) has folded. The pot is currently 150 (100 from Flashlight's Big Blind and 50 from my Small Blind). I need to call 50 more to see a flop. K7o is a relatively strong hand in a blind-versus-blind situation.
